class_schedule.topic: What Hiring Managers Look For in UX Portfolios
learning_objectives: Identify the qualities that distinguish strong portfolios from weak ones
Understand how UX portfolios are evaluated during hiring processes
Analyze portfolio patterns from successful UX designers at various career levels
readings: Refactoring your UX Portfolio (NNg research report)
The UX of UX Portfolios (article by Sarah Doody)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your portfolio is the most important artifact in your UX career. It is not a gallery of pretty screens — it is a demonstration of your thinking process, problem-solving ability, and impact.
key_concepts: Process over polish: hiring managers care about HOW you solved the problem
Case study structure: context, problem, process, solution, impact
Showing your role: what YOU did vs. what the team did
Quantified impact: measurable outcomes beat subjective claims
Portfolio curation: 3-4 strong case studies beat 10 shallow ones
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side analysis of two UX portfolios — one that shows beautiful screens and one that shows messy process with clear thinking — discussing which gets the interview and why
setup_requirements: Portfolio platform account (Notion, Webflow, or personal site)

review.value: Hiring managers want to see how you think: research findings that changed direction, sketches that explored alternatives, test results that drove iteration. Pretty mockups alone show you can use Figma, not that you can solve problems.
'Redesigned the checkout page' is weak. 'Reduced checkout abandonment from 68% to 41% by simplifying the payment form from 12 fields to 5, validated through A/B testing with 10,000 users' is strong.

learning_objectives: Write case study narratives that engage hiring managers
Select and present design artifacts that demonstrate thinking, not just output
Frame challenges and constraints honestly without making excuses
readings: Articulating Design Decisions by Tom Greever, Ch. 6-8
How to write a UX case study (InVision article)

lecture_notes.introduction: A case study is a story of problem-solving. The best ones follow a narrative arc that makes the reader care about the problem, appreciate the process, and respect the outcome.
key_concepts: Narrative arc: hook (the problem), rising action (research and discovery), climax (the key insight), resolution (the solution), denouement (the impact)
Artifact selection: show messy reality not sanitized deliverables
Constraint framing: deadlines, budgets, and scope limitations show real-world skills
The role statement: explicitly stating what you contributed in team projects
Visual storytelling: using screenshots, annotations, and before/after comparisons
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshopping a case study together — taking a raw project description and structuring it into a compelling narrative with artifact selection

review.value: Open with the problem, not the solution: 'Users were abandoning the onboarding flow at a 78% rate, costing the company an estimated $2.1M in annual revenue.' This makes the reader care immediately.
Saying 'I had 2 weeks and no research budget so I ran 5 guerrilla interviews at a coffee shop' shows resourcefulness and real-world skill, not weakness.

learning_objectives: Select and build a portfolio platform that showcases your work effectively
Design the portfolio itself as a UX artifact — applying your own design skills
Optimize portfolio performance, accessibility, and mobile responsiveness
readings: Don't Make Me Think by Steve Krug, Ch. 1-3 (applied to portfolio UX)
Web Portfolio Design: Best Practices (Smashing Magazine)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your portfolio IS a UX project. If it is hard to navigate, slow to load, or inaccessible, it contradicts the skills you claim to have. Apply everything you have learned in this program to your own portfolio.
key_concepts: Platform options: custom site (Webflow, Next.js), templates (Squarespace), document (Notion, PDF)
Portfolio IA: clear navigation between case studies, about, resume, contact
Loading performance: image optimization, lazy loading, fast initial render
Mobile-first: recruiters often first see your portfolio on their phone
Accessibility: your portfolio must be accessible — it is your first work sample
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live portfolio build session — setting up a portfolio structure in Webflow with responsive layout, optimized images, and accessible navigation

review.value: If a recruiter cannot find your case studies in 5 seconds, your portfolio has a navigation problem. If it takes 10 seconds to load, you have a performance problem. Practice what you preach.
Recruiters browse LinkedIn on their phone, click your portfolio link, and judge in 10 seconds. If your portfolio is not responsive and fast on mobile, you lose before they read anything.

learning_objectives: Conduct portfolio reviews using structured critique frameworks
Identify and fix common portfolio weaknesses
Iterate on portfolios based on peer and industry feedback
readings: Design is a Job by Mike Monteiro, Ch. on Presenting Work
Portfolio Peer Review Best Practices (UXPA resource)

lecture_notes.introduction: Portfolios are living documents that improve through critique and iteration — the same design thinking you apply to products. Today we critique each other's portfolios with the rigor of a usability study.
key_concepts: Portfolio critique framework: first impression, navigation, case study depth, visual design, accessibility
Common weaknesses: too many projects, no process shown, missing outcomes, poor writing
Industry-specific portfolios: tailoring emphasis for product design, research, or interaction roles
The about page: telling your professional story authentically
Portfolio testing: watching recruiters browse your portfolio and noting where they stop
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured portfolio review workshop — pairs exchange portfolios, evaluate using the critique framework, and provide written feedback

review.value: Show your portfolio to someone for 10 seconds and ask: What do I do? What are my strengths? Can you find a case study? If they cannot answer these questions, the portfolio needs restructuring.

Product design roles want to see end-to-end process. Research roles want to see methodology rigor. Interaction design roles want to see prototyping depth. Tailor case study emphasis to the role you are targeting.

learning_objectives: Present a portfolio case study in a design interview format
Handle interviewer questions about design decisions and trade-offs
Practice whiteboard and take-home design challenges
readings: Cracking the PM Interview by Gayle McDowell, Ch. on Design Questions
Preparing for UX Design Interviews (NNg article)

lecture_notes.introduction: The portfolio gets you the interview. Your presentation skills determine if you get the offer. Practicing how you present your work and respond to challenges is as important as the work itself.
key_concepts: Portfolio presentation structure: 15 minutes, one case study, Q&A
Anticipating questions: why not alternative approaches? what would you do differently? how did you handle disagreement?
Whiteboard challenge preparation: structured frameworks for real-time design thinking
Take-home challenge strategies: scoping, time management, and presentation
Articulating design rationale: connecting every decision to user needs or business goals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock interview round — students present one case study in 15 minutes followed by Q&A from a panel of classmates and the instructor acting as hiring managers

review.value: For every design decision in your case study, you should be able to answer: Why this approach? What alternatives did you consider? What evidence supported this choice? If you cannot answer, the decision appears arbitrary.

Always have a genuine, thoughtful answer. It shows self-awareness and growth. 'I would have conducted more research upfront — we were constrained by timeline but I now see how that led to a pivot in sprint 3 that cost us a week.'

questions.question: A junior UX designer's portfolio has 8 case studies. The first three show only final screens with no process. The last five show detailed process but the writing is dense and unformatted. What specific advice would you give?
questions.answer: Advice: (1) Cut to 3-4 case studies total — quality over quantity. Keep the ones with the richest process documentation. (2) For each remaining case study, add process sections: research findings, sketches, wireframes, test results, iterations. Even if original artifacts are lost, reconstruct the narrative of what was learned at each stage. (3) Fix the writing: break dense text into scannable sections with clear headings, use bullet points for key findings, limit paragraphs to 3-4 lines, and lead each section with the key insight. (4) Add measurable outcomes wherever possible — task success rates, conversion improvements, time-on-task reduction. (5) Add a clear role statement to each project specifying personal contributions.

rubric.key: Recommends reducing to fewer, stronger case studies
Identifies the need to add process to polished screens
Addresses writing quality with specific formatting advice
Recommends measurable outcomes and role clarity

questions.question: Write the opening paragraph (hook) for a case study about redesigning a university course registration system that students found confusing.
questions.answer: Example hook: 'Every semester, 12,000 students at State University navigated a course registration system so confusing that the IT help desk received over 800 support tickets during the first week of registration alone. Students missed enrollment deadlines, accidentally registered for wrong sections, and senior advisors spent 30% of their time helping students fix registration errors instead of providing academic guidance. I was asked to redesign the registration experience to reduce these pain points — with a 6-week timeline before the next enrollment period.' This hook works because: it quantifies the problem (800 tickets, 12,000 students), identifies who is affected (students, advisors, IT), establishes stakes (missed deadlines), and sets a constraint (6 weeks).

rubric.key: Opens with the problem, not the solution
Creates urgency or stakes that make the reader care

questions.question: During a design interview, the interviewer asks: 'Why did you choose card sorting over tree testing for your IA research?' How would you structure your answer?
questions.answer: Structure using the STAR-like framework for design rationale: (1) Context: 'We were redesigning the navigation for a content-heavy site with 200+ pages. The existing IA was based on the organization's internal structure, which did not match user expectations.' (2) Rationale: 'I chose open card sorting because we needed to discover how users naturally categorize the content — we did not have a hypothesis about the right structure yet. Tree testing validates an existing structure; card sorting generates one.' (3) Outcome: 'The card sort with 15 participants revealed 4 major category groupings that differed significantly from the existing navigation. This directly informed the new IA.' (4) Reflection: 'After implementing the new IA, I did run a tree test to validate findability — card sorting and tree testing are complementary, not competing methods.'

rubric.key: Explains the rationale with method comparison
Describes the outcome with specific findings

questions.question: An interviewer shows you a poorly designed mobile screen and asks you to critique it in 2 minutes. What framework would you use to structure your critique?
questions.answer: Framework for rapid critique: (1) First impression (5 seconds): What is the purpose of this screen? Can I tell immediately? If not, there is a hierarchy problem. (2) Information architecture: Is the content organized logically? Are related elements grouped? Is the navigation clear? (3) Visual design: Does the hierarchy guide my eye correctly? Are touch targets adequate? Is text readable? (4) Interaction design: What am I supposed to do here? Is the primary action obvious? Are interactive elements discoverable? (5) Accessibility: Is the contrast sufficient? Would this work with one hand? Could a screen reader navigate it? Structure the response as: top 3 issues prioritized by user impact, with a specific fix proposed for each. Do not try to catch everything — demonstrate structured thinking and prioritization.

rubric.key: Uses a structured framework, not random observations
Proposes specific fixes, not just problems

major_assignments.title: Professional UX Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Build a complete, interview-ready UX portfolio with 3-4 case studies, an about page, and resume. The portfolio must be responsive, accessible, and demonstrate the UX skills claimed in the case studies.
objectives: Curate and write 3-4 compelling case studies with measurable outcomes
Build a portfolio platform that is responsive, accessible, and performant
Practice and refine portfolio presentation for interview contexts
Iterate based on peer, instructor, and industry professional feedback
deliverables: Live portfolio website or document accessible via URL
3-4 case studies following the narrative arc structure
About page with professional story and resume/CV
Accessibility audit of your own portfolio

criteria: Each case study follows the narrative arc: problem, process, solution, impact
Process is visible: research, sketches, iterations, testing
Measurable outcomes or impact stated where possible
Your role is clearly articulated in team projects
Navigation is clear and case studies are findable in under 5 seconds
Responsive design works on mobile, tablet, and desktop
Loading performance is fast
Accessibility meets WCAG 2.1 AA
Writing is clear, scannable, and professional
Headings and structure guide the reader through each case study
Technical terms are used correctly
Grammar and spelling are polished
15-minute presentation is well-structured and engaging
Design rationale is articulated for key decisions
Questions are handled with confidence and reflection
Presentation demonstrates communication skills
Compelling case studies demonstrating process, thinking, and impact
Portfolio platform that practices what it preaches (responsive, accessible, fast)
Professional writing quality with clear narrative structure
Strong presentation skills with articulate design rationale

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on portfolio strategy, case study writing, and presentation skills

case_study_drafts.description: Progressive case study drafts with instructor and peer feedback

portfolio_project.description: Complete professional portfolio with case studies and presentation

peer_reviews.description: Structured portfolio reviews of classmates' work

mock_interviews.description: Mock design interview presentations with industry professional feedback

course_policies.attendance: Workshop and critique sessions are mandatory. Portfolio improvement depends on giving and receiving feedback. More than 2 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Case study drafts must be submitted on time for peer review. Late drafts miss feedback cycles and lose 15% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is required and graded. All portfolio content must be your own work. Having someone else write your case studies is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All case study content must represent your actual work and role. Inflating your contributions on team projects or fabricating metrics is grounds for failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Portfolio platform of choice (Webflow, Squarespace, Notion, or custom), image editing software for optimizing portfolio images, and screen recording for presentation practice.
